title: Диспетчеризация интерфейсного вызова: виртуальность, таблица методов, EIMI

cards.prompt: Both Name() methods are explicit interface implementations (EIMI) and thus private, reachable only via a cast to their own interface: (IArtist)p dispatches to IArtist.Name ("artist"), (IChef)p to IChef.Name ("chef"). A plain p.Name() would not even compile. So it prints artist chef
cards.code: using System; interface IArtist { string Name(); } interface IChef { string Name(); } class Person : IArtist, IChef { string IArtist.Name() => "artist"; string IChef.Name() => "chef"; } var p = new Person(); Console.WriteLine($"{((IArtist)p).Name()} {((IChef)p).Name()}");

cards.prompt: A public member and an EIMI are two distinct method-table slots. st.Dispose() calls the public version; ((IDisposable)st).Dispose() calls the private explicit implementation. The compiler marks the EIMI private, so it prints public Dispose IDisposable Dispose
cards.code: using System; class SimpleType : IDisposable { public void Dispose() => Console.Write("public Dispose "); void IDisposable.Dispose() => Console.Write("IDisposable Dispose"); } var st = new SimpleType(); st.Dispose(); ((IDisposable)st).Dispose();

cards.prompt: Interface calls are virtual: x.Area() dispatches to the implementation bound to each object's own type. Sq.Area()=4, Ci.Area()=3, so the sum is 7 — polymorphism through the interface, not the variable type
cards.code: using System; using System.Collections.Generic; interface IShape { double Area(); } class Sq : IShape { public double Area() => 4; } class Ci : IShape { public double Area() => 3; } var shapes = new List<IShape>{ new Sq(), new Ci() }; double s = 0; foreach (var x in shapes) s += x.Area(); Console.WriteLine(s);
